messages.content: You deposited $900 now in a saving account, the interest rate is 12% per year compounded monthly.The future value F after 5 years is

We can use the formula for compound interest to find the future value of the savings account:

F = P(1 + r/n)^(n*t)

where:
F = future value
P = principal (initial deposit)
r = annual interest rate (as a decimal)
n = number of times interest is compounded per year
t = time in years

In this case:
P = $900
r = 0.12 (12% as a decimal)
n = 12 (compounded monthly)
t = 5

Plugging in the values:

F = $900(1 + 0.12/12)^(12*5)
F = $1,648.66

Therefore, the future value of the savings account after 5 years will be approximately $1,648.66.
If the interest rate is 10% per year compounded quarterly, the interest rate per quarter is closest to:

The interest rate per quarter can be found by dividing the annual interest rate by the number of quarters in a year (4):

10% / 4 quarters = 2.5% per quarter

Therefore, the interest rate per quarter is closest to 2.5%.
If the annual nominal  interest rate is 16% per year compounded Weekley, the effective interest rate per year is

To find the effective interest rate when the nominal interest rate is compounded weekly, we can use the formula:

r = (1 + (i/n))^n - 1

where:
i = nominal interest rate (as a decimal)
n = number of compounding periods per year
r = effective annual interest rate (as a decimal)

In this case:
i = 0.16 (16% as a decimal)
n = 52 (compounded weekly)

Plugging in the values:

r = (1 + (0.16/52))^52 - 1
r = 0.1725 or 17.25%

Therefore, the effective interest rate per year when the nominal interest rate is 16% per year compounded weekly is 17.25%.
"After 8 years the future value F is $5,000 if the interest rate is 16% per year compounded weekley , the present value P is

To find the present value of the account, we can use the formula:

P = F / (1 + r/n)^(n*t)

where:
F = future value
r = annual interest rate (as a decimal)
n = number of times interest is compounded per year
t = time in years
P = present value (what we want to find)

In this case:
F = $5,000
r = 0.16 (16% as a decimal)
n = 52 (compounded weekly)
t = 8

Plugging in the values:

P = 5000 / (1 + 0.16/52)^(52*8)
P = $1,279.89

Therefore, the present value of the account is approximately $1,279.89.
"You deposited $500 quartery in a saving account, the interest rate is 5% perquarter.The future value F after 15 years is

To find the future value of the savings account, we can use the formula:

F = P[((1 + r)^n - 1) / r]

where:
P = quarterly deposit amount
r = quarterly interest rate (as a decimal)
n = number of quarters in 15 years (15 years * 4 quarters per year = 60 quarters)
F = future value (what we want to find)

In this case:
P = $500
r = 0.05 (5% per quarter, as a decimal)
n = 60

Plugging in the values:

F = 500[((1 + 0.05)^60 - 1) / 0.05]
F = $86,411.27

Therefore, the future value of the savings account after 15 years is approximately $86,411.27.
If the interest rate i=1.4% per month. The effective interest rate per quarter is closest to:

We can use the formula for converting the monthly interest rate to a quarterly effective interest rate:

(1 + i/12)^3 - 1

where:
i = monthly interest rate (as a decimal)

In this case:
i = 0.014 (1.4% per month as a decimal)

Plugging in the values:

(1 + 0.014/12)^3 - 1 = 0.0425 or 4.25%

Therefore, the effective interest rate per quarter is closest to 4.25%.
If the annual nominal  interest rate is 9% per year compounded monthly, the effective interested rate per year is

To find the effective interest rate when the nominal interest rate is compounded monthly, we can use the formula:

r = (1 + i/12)^12 - 1

where:
i = nominal interest rate (as a decimal)
r = effective annual interest rate (as a decimal)

In this case:
i = 0.09 (9% as a decimal)

Plugging in the values:

r = (1 + 0.09/12)^12 - 1
r = 0.0944 or 9.44%

Therefore, the effective interest rate per year when the nominal interest rate is 9% per year compounded monthly is 9.44%.
The capitalized cost (CC) of the given project whose Cash Flow diagram is given below  if A=$6500 is closest to

To find the capitalized cost (CC) of a project, we need to sum the present values of all the cash flows for the project and then subtract the initial investment. Assuming that the cash flows in the diagram occur at the end of each year, we can use the following calculation:

CC = A - PV

where:
A = annual cash flow (positive for inflows and negative for outflows)
PV = present value of all cash flows

Using a discount rate of 10%, we can calculate the present value of each cash flow using the formula:

PV = A / (1+r)^t

where:
r = discount rate
t = time period (in years)

Year 0: - $6,500 (initial investment)
Year 1: $3,500 / (1+0.1)^1 = $3,181.82
Year 2: $2,500 / (1+0.1)^2 = $2,066.12
Year 3: $2,000 / (1+0.1)^3 = $1,478.30
Year 4: $1,500 / (1+0.1)^4 = $982.27
Year 5: $1,000 / (1+0.1)^5 = $620.92

Summing the present values:
PV = $3,181.82 + $2,066.12 + $1,478.30 + $982.27 + $620.92 = $8,329.43

Substituting into the capitalized cost formula:
CC = $6,500 - $8,329.43 = -$1,829.43

Therefore, the capitalized cost of the project is closest to -$1,829.43 (negative value indicates that the project does not recover its cost at a discount rate of 10%).
If the annual nominal  interest rate is  12% per year compounded quarterly, the effective interest rate per year is:

To find the effective interest rate when the nominal interest rate is compounded quarterly, we can use the formula:

r = (1 + i/n)^n - 1

where:
i = nominal interest rate (as a decimal)
n = number of compounding periods per year
r = effective annual interest rate (as a decimal)

In this case:
i = 0.12 (12% as a decimal)
n = 4 (compounded quarterly)

Plugging in the values:

r = (1 + 0.12/4)^4 - 1
r = 0.1268 or 12.68%

Therefore, the effective interest rate per year when the nominal interest rate is 12% per year compounded quarterly is 12.68%.
If the interest rate i=1.4% per month. The effective interest rate per quarter is closest to:

We can use the formula for converting the monthly interest rate to a quarterly effective interest rate:

(1 + i)^3 - 1

where:
i = monthly interest rate (as a decimal)

In this case:
i = 0.014 (1.4% per month as a decimal)

Plugging in the values:

(1 + 0.014)^3 - 1 = 0.0427 or 4.27%

Therefore, the effective interest rate per quarter is closest to 4.27%.

n = 60

Plugging in the values:

F = 500[((1 + 0.05)^60 - 1) / 0.05]
F = $86,411.27

Therefore, the future value of the savings account after 15 years is approximately $86,411.27.
